title: Economics
intro: Economics questions center on macroeconomic theory and the economists who built it, with John Maynard Keynes, Milton Friedman, and David Ricardo among the most frequently asked answers. Business cycles, inflation, money supply, and unemployment recur constantly as both standalone answerlines and as the subject of theories attributed to named schools of thought—Keynesian, monetarist, Austrian, and new classical. Growth theory, centered on the Solow-Swan model, and the tools of monetary and fiscal policy (interest rates, quantitative easing, government spending) round out the macroeconomic core.

Microeconomic canon is built around utility, demand, and market structure: oligopoly, monopoly, monopolistic competition, and monopsony appear as a coherent family of market-structure answers, while indifference curves, marginal utility, and the Slutsky decomposition test consumer-theory fundamentals. Game theory contributes a dense cluster of its own—Nash equilibrium, the prisoner's dilemma, auctions, and mechanism design—overlapping with information economics (adverse selection, moral hazard, signaling) associated with Akerlof, Stiglitz, and Stigler.

A second major cluster covers named economists whose contributions double as concepts: Ronald Coase and the Coase theorem, Vilfredo Pareto and Pareto efficiency, Kenneth Arrow and general equilibrium, and Amartya Sen's welfare and development economics. Financial economics forms a distinct pocket—options, the Black-Scholes model, the Capital Asset Pricing Model, and risk/volatility—as does behavioral economics, anchored by Daniel Kahneman, Richard Thaler, and Prospect Theory. International trade and development (comparative advantage, exchange rates, the Heckscher-Ohlin model, microfinance) and labor/welfare economics (human capital, minimum wage, inequality measures like the Gini coefficient) each supply a further tier of recurring answers.

Beyond the highest-frequency answerlines, the canon has a long tail of specific terms, models, and applied econometric methods—instrumental variables, natural experiments, difference-in-differences—reflecting the field's empirical turn, alongside classical-era concepts (the water-diamond paradox, Malthusian population theory, Ricardian rent) that persist as identifiers even when asked only a handful of times.

sections.name: Classical & Neoclassical Foundations
sections.blurb: The classical and neoclassical economists and doctrines—from Ricardo and Malthus through Marshall, Jevons, Pareto, and Marx—that first formalized value, rent, and general-equilibrium theory.

entries.note: Nobel economist known for the Arrow-Debreu general equilibrium model and Arrow's Impossibility Theorem on aggregating voter preferences.
Neoclassical economist who formalized supply-and-demand curves and marginal analysis in Principles of Economics (1890).
Italian economist-sociologist known for Pareto efficiency, the 80/20 Pareto principle, and elite-circulation theory in sociology.
Marginalist economist who co-founded marginal utility theory and wrote on the paradox of value and business cycles.
Classical economist who argued population grows geometrically while food supply grows arithmetically, in An Essay on the Principle of Population.
Payment to a factor of production above what is needed to keep it in its current use; central to Ricardian rent theory.
German philosopher-economist whose Das Kapital critiqued capitalism via the labor theory of value and theory of exploitation.
Economic system of collective or state ownership of the means of production, contrasted with capitalism in Marxist and classical debate.
Institutional economist who coined conspicuous consumption in The Theory of the Leisure Class, critiquing status-driven spending.

entries.topic: An Essay on the Principle of Population
entries.answerline: An Essay on the Principle of Population
entries.note: Thomas Malthus's 1798 work arguing population grows faster than food supply, leading to recurring subsistence crises.
Direct exchange of goods without money, the classical starting point for theories explaining money's origin as a medium of exchange.
Social science studying how individuals and societies allocate scarce resources among competing uses, per Lionel Robbins's classic definition.
Home to French economists such as Cournot, Walras, and Piketty, and to the Physiocratic school preceding classical economics.
Analysis of how supply and demand interact simultaneously across all markets in an economy; formalized by Walras and by Arrow and Debreu.
Mathematical economist who, with Kenneth Arrow, proved the existence of general equilibrium; won the Nobel for Theory of Value.
German economist whose Isolated State (1826) modeled agricultural land use as concentric rings based on transport cost to a central market.
Centrally planned command economy contrasted with market capitalism in classical and Cold War-era economic debate.
Full name of Thorstein Veblen, institutional economist who coined conspicuous consumption in The Theory of the Leisure Class.
Central to the classical paradox of value, where useful water is cheap while less-useful diamonds are costly, resolved by marginal utility.

sections.name: Macroeconomic Theory & Business Cycles
sections.blurb: Keynesian, monetarist, and new classical accounts of growth, inflation, unemployment, and the monetary and fiscal levers used to manage the business cycle.

entries.note: British economist whose General Theory (1936) founded macroeconomics, advocating government spending to fight unemployment during recessions.
Long-run increase in an economy's output, studied via Solow-Swan and endogenous growth models emphasizing capital, labor, and technology.
Chicago School monetarist who argued money-supply growth drives inflation and business cycles; wrote A Monetary History of the United States.
Recurring fluctuations of expansion and recession in aggregate output, explained by Keynesian, monetarist, and real-business-cycle theories.
Sustained rise in the general price level, explained by monetarist money-supply growth, demand-pull, and cost-push theories.
Medium of exchange whose supply and velocity link to inflation and output in quantity-theory and Keynesian frameworks.
Central-bank-controlled stock of currency and deposits (M1/M2); monetarists tie its growth rate directly to inflation.
Price of borrowing money, set in part by central banks as a monetary-policy tool to influence investment and inflation.
Founder of the neoclassical synthesis blending Keynesian and neoclassical economics; wrote the influential textbook Economics.
Share of the labor force without work, decomposed into frictional, structural, and cyclical types tied to the business cycle.
Tradition of Menger, Mises, and Hayek emphasizing subjective value and an Austrian business-cycle theory blaming credit expansion for booms and busts.
Household spending component of GDP; Keynes's consumption function and Friedman's permanent-income hypothesis model its determinants.
New classical economist behind the Lucas critique and rational-expectations macroeconomics; won the Nobel Prize in 1995.
Austrian School economist who warned of central planning in The Road to Serfdom and developed Austrian business-cycle theory.
Economist who developed national-income accounting for GDP and the Kuznets curve linking growth and inequality.
Percentage of the labor force actively seeking work; tracked alongside inflation via the Phillips curve tradeoff.
Borrowed funds owed by governments, firms, or households; public debt and deficits are central fiscal-policy concerns.
Percentage change in the price level over time; targeted by central banks via monetary policy.
Economist who coined creative destruction to describe entrepreneurial innovation continually displacing incumbent firms and technologies.
New classical theory attributing business cycles to technology shocks rather than monetary or demand disturbances.
Portion of income not consumed; determines capital accumulation in Solow-Swan growth models via the savings rate.
Total planned spending in an economy across consumption, investment, government, and net exports; its shortfall Keynes blamed for recessions.
Global-warming economics, including carbon pricing and discounting future damages, associated with William Nordhaus's integrated assessment models.
Schumpeter's term for innovation-driven displacement of old firms, products, and jobs by new ones, fueling long-run growth.
Cost of borrowing set partly by central-bank policy; influences investment, saving, and exchange rates.
Nobel economist known for Tobin's q investment theory and the proposed Tobin tax on international currency transactions.
New classical assumption that agents form forecasts using all available information, underlying Robert Lucas's critique of policy models.
Neoclassical growth model where capital accumulation, labor, and exogenous technology determine long-run output per worker.
Nobel economist who integrated climate change into long-run growth models, pioneering environmental macroeconomics.
Rate used to convert future values to present value, and the rate the Federal Reserve charges banks for direct loans.
Nobel economist known for the life-cycle hypothesis of saving and, with Miller, the Modigliani-Miller theorem on capital structure.
Short-term joblessness from workers transitioning between jobs or entering the labor force, distinct from structural or cyclical unemployment.
Gross Domestic Product: total market value of goods and services produced in an economy, the standard measure of output.
Fiscal-policy component of aggregate demand; Keynesians advocate raising it during recessions to stimulate output and employment.
Asset and market whose booms and busts, as in 2008, transmit through mortgage credit into broader business cycles.
Central-bank actions adjusting interest rates and the money supply to manage inflation and employment.
Full name of Paul Samuelson, founder of the neoclassical synthesis and author of the textbook Economics.
Empirical relationship suggesting a tradeoff between inflation and unemployment, later modified to include expectations.
National-income accounting framework, including GDP and GNP, used to measure an economy's aggregate output and flows.
Home of the Austrian School of economics, including Menger, Mises, Hayek, and Schumpeter, known for subjective value and business-cycle theory.
Interest rate U.S. banks charge each other for overnight loans; the Federal Reserve's primary monetary-policy tool.
Austrian School economist known for The Road to Serfdom and Austrian business-cycle theory blaming credit expansion for booms and busts.
Harvard economist and textbook author known for New Keynesian menu-cost models and popular Principles of Economics texts.
Extremely rapid, out-of-control price increases, typically caused by excessive money creation to finance government deficits.
Economist who served as Federal Reserve Chair and later U.S. Treasury Secretary, known for labor-market and monetary-policy research.
Economy associated with the 1990s Lost Decade of stagnation and deflation following its asset-price bubble collapse.
Costs of changing posted prices, used in New Keynesian models to explain short-run price stickiness and non-neutral monetary policy.
Heterodox framework arguing sovereign currency issuers face no solvency constraint and should use fiscal policy to manage inflation.
Classical proposition that changes in the money supply affect only nominal variables, not real output, in the long run.
Empirical relationship between GDP growth and changes in the unemployment rate, formulated by Arthur Okun.
Unconventional monetary policy where a central bank buys long-term securities to lower long-term rates when short-term rates are near zero.
Periods of declining economic activity, typically defined as two consecutive quarters of falling GDP.
New classical economist behind rational expectations and the Lucas critique; won the 1995 Nobel Prize.
Nobel economist whose Solow growth model attributes long-run per-capita growth mainly to exogenous technological progress.
Portion of income not spent on consumption; fuels investment and capital accumulation in growth models.
Share of income saved rather than consumed; a key determinant of steady-state capital per worker in the Solow model.
Neoclassical growth model, developed independently by Solow and Swan, where capital accumulation and exogenous technology drive long-run output.
Term for self-fulfilling, extrinsic beliefs that can generate business-cycle fluctuations in models with multiple equilibria.
Exogenous driver of long-run growth in the Solow model, whose contribution is captured by total factor productivity.
Portion of output growth not explained by increases in capital or labor, typically attributed to technological progress.

sections.name: Consumer & Producer Theory
sections.blurb: The microeconomic building blocks of utility, demand, cost, and production that describe how households and firms make marginal choices.

entries.note: Measure of satisfaction from consumption underlying demand theory; cardinal and ordinal versions support indifference-curve analysis.
Factor of production alongside land and labor, encompassing physical plant and equipment and its accumulation in growth models.
Point where quantity supplied equals quantity demanded, clearing a market at a stable price.
Curves showing consumption bundles yielding equal utility; their convex shape reflects diminishing marginal rate of substitution.
Signals coordinating supply and demand decisions, set at the intersection of supply and demand curves under competition.
Quantity of a good buyers wish to purchase at each price, graphed as a downward-sloping curve reflecting diminishing marginal utility.
Cost of producing one additional unit; profit-maximizing firms set output where marginal cost equals marginal revenue.
Amount paid for a good, determined by supply-and-demand equilibrium under perfect competition.
Consumer response to relative price changes, replacing a costlier good with a cheaper substitute; part of the Slutsky decomposition.
Intertemporal dimension of choice, underlying discounting, time preference, and the tradeoff between present and future consumption.
Value of the next-best alternative forgone when making a choice; central to marginal decision-making in economics.
Measure of how much quantity demanded changes in response to a price change, distinguishing elastic from inelastic goods.
Payment for the use of a factor of production, notably land, distinguished from economic rent as surplus above opportunity cost.
Decomposes a price change's effect on demand into substitution and income effects; named for Eugen Slutsky.
General measure of responsiveness of one variable to another, such as quantity demanded to price or income.
Measure of how easily one input or good can be substituted for another as relative prices change.
Curve showing maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce given fixed resources, illustrating opportunity cost.
Mathematical representations of consumer preferences ranking bundles of goods, used to derive demand curves.
Production function property where output scales exactly proportionally with all inputs, so doubling capital and labor doubles output.
Inputs to production, traditionally land, labor, and capital, sometimes entrepreneurship, combined via a production function to yield output.
Rare goods for which quantity demanded rises as price rises, because a strong negative income effect outweighs the substitution effect.
Change in quantity demanded resulting from a change in real purchasing power caused by a price change, per the Slutsky decomposition.
Production function property where output grows more than proportionally to input increases, often driving natural monopoly.
Classical factor of production encompassing natural resources; its fixed supply underlies Ricardian and Georgist rent theory.
Non-work time valued in utility, traded off against labor income in standard labor-supply models.
Prefix for concepts like marginal cost, marginal utility, and marginal product, describing the effect of one additional unit.
Additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit of a good; diminishes as consumption increases, per the law of diminishing marginal utility.
Process of combining inputs such as land, labor, and capital to create goods and services, modeled via production functions.
Mathematical relationships mapping input quantities like labor and capital to output, such as the Cobb-Douglas form.

sections.name: Market Structure & Industrial Organization
sections.blurb: How competition, market power, and strategic pricing vary across monopoly, oligopoly, monopolistic competition, and monopsony.

entries.note: Market structure with few interdependent sellers, modeled via Cournot quantity competition or Bertrand price competition.
Statistician-economist known for Hotelling's law of spatial competition and Hotelling's rule governing nonrenewable-resource extraction.
Market structure of many firms selling differentiated products with free entry, yielding downward-sloping demand and excess capacity.
Market structure with a single seller facing the whole market demand curve, restricting output to raise price above marginal cost.
Market structure with a single buyer, notably applied to labor markets where employers can suppress wages below marginal product.
Firm strategy to shift demand and signal quality under monopolistic competition and imperfect information.
Falling average cost as output rises, driving natural monopoly and industry concentration in some sectors.
Business organizations whose boundaries Coase explained via transaction costs; theory of the firm studies their production decisions.
British economist who pioneered the theory of imperfect competition and introduced the term monopsony for single-buyer markets.
19th-century French mathematician whose Cournot model describes quantity-setting duopolists reaching a stable equilibrium.
Ability of a firm to set prices above marginal cost, ranging from limited under monopolistic competition to unconstrained under monopoly.
Phenomenon where a good's value to users increases as more people use it, driving winner-take-all dynamics in platform markets.
Likely shorthand for perfect competition, the market structure of many price-taking firms with free entry and exit.
Charging different prices to different customers for the same good based on willingness to pay, requiring market power and no resale.

sections.name: Market Failure & Public Economics
sections.blurb: Externalities, public goods, taxation, and collective-action problems that cause markets to diverge from efficient outcomes, and the policies designed to correct them.

entries.note: Non-excludable, non-rival goods like national defense that markets underprovide, motivating government provision and free-rider analysis.
Economist behind the Coase theorem and the transaction-cost theory of the firm, explaining why firms exist and how externalities can be resolved.
Uncompensated third-party costs or benefits from an economic activity, addressed via Pigouvian taxes or Coasean bargaining.
Government levies used to raise revenue, correct externalities via Pigouvian taxes, and redistribute income.
Costs of negotiating, monitoring, and enforcing exchanges; central to Coase's theory of the firm and property-rights bargaining.
Public-choice topic covering majority rule and strategic voting, tied to Arrow's Impossibility Theorem for aggregating individual preferences.
Market-based environmental policy capping total emissions and letting firms trade pollution permits to reach efficient abatement.
Shared resources vulnerable to overuse absent clear property rights, as in Garrett Hardin's tragedy-of-the-commons framing.
Loss of economic efficiency when equilibrium is not achieved, caused by taxes, price controls, or monopoly pricing.
Formal and informal rules such as property rights, law, and norms shaping economic behavior, central to New Institutional Economics.
Garrett Hardin's concept that shared, unregulated resources get overexploited because individual users bear only part of the cost.
British economist who developed welfare economics and proposed Pigouvian taxes to correct negative externalities like pollution.
Ronald Coase's proposition that with well-defined property rights and no transaction costs, parties bargain to an efficient outcome regardless of initial allocation.
Tendency for individuals to benefit from a public good without contributing to its cost, motivating collective-action analysis.
19th-century economist who proposed a single tax on land value in Progress and Poverty, founding the Georgist movement.
Economist whose The Logic of Collective Action explained why large groups struggle to organize for shared interests, due to free-riding.
Public-choice model predicting that majority-rule voting outcomes converge to the preference of the median voter.
Uncompensated costs imposed on third parties, such as pollution, corrected via Pigouvian taxes or cap-and-trade schemes.
Phenomenon where regulatory agencies come to serve the interests of the industries they regulate rather than the public, per George Stigler.
Price ceiling on rents intended to keep housing affordable, criticized by economists for reducing housing supply and quality.
Full name of Ronald Coase, known for the Coase theorem and transaction-cost theory of the firm.

sections.name: Game Theory & Information Economics
sections.blurb: Strategic interaction, auctions, and the economics of asymmetric information, from Nash equilibrium to adverse selection and signaling.

entries.note: Asymmetric or imperfect information distorts markets, studied via adverse selection, signaling, and screening models.
Mechanisms for allocating goods via bidding; English, Dutch, first-price sealed-bid, and Vickrey second-price formats are studied in auction theory.
Strategy profile where no player benefits from unilaterally deviating; foundational solution concept named for John Nash.
Classic game where mutual defection is the dominant strategy despite cooperation being jointly better, illustrating collective-action failure.
Nobel economist known for screening models and the economics of asymmetric information, alongside Akerlof and Spence.
Mathematical study of strategic decision-making among interdependent agents, formalized by von Neumann, Morgenstern, and Nash.
Tendency to take more risk once insured or protected from consequences, a key information-economics problem in insurance markets.
Situation where one party has more or better information than another, causing adverse selection and moral hazard.
Agreements structuring economic exchange under incomplete information; contract theory studies incentives and principal-agent problems.
Formal strategic-interaction structures with players, strategies, and payoffs analyzed in game theory, from zero-sum to cooperative games.
Reverse game theory: designing rules or institutions, like auctions, so self-interested play yields a desired social outcome.
Simple, successful strategy in repeated prisoner's-dilemma tournaments: cooperate first, then mirror the opponent's previous move.
Coordination game where two players prefer to coordinate but disagree on which of two outcomes to coordinate on.
Nobel economist whose Market for Lemons paper founded the study of adverse selection from information asymmetry.
Chicago School economist known for the economics of information, search theory, and the theory of regulatory capture.
Risk-pooling market plagued by adverse selection and moral hazard, a classic example in information economics.
Plural of Nash equilibrium: strategy profiles where no player can benefit by unilaterally changing strategy.
Costly action taken to credibly convey private information, as in Michael Spence's job-market signaling model using education.
Bargaining game where one player proposes splitting a sum and the other can accept or reject, testing fairness against pure self-interest.
Games where one player's gain exactly equals another's loss, contrasted with positive-sum or cooperative games.

sections.name: Behavioral Economics
sections.blurb: The psychology of economic decision-making, including bounded rationality, cognitive biases, and departures from the rational-actor model.

entries.note: Kahneman and Tversky's model of decision-making under risk, featuring loss aversion and reference-dependent value functions.
Assumption that agents make consistent, self-interested choices; behavioral economics documents systematic departures from it.
Field combining psychology and economics to study cognitive biases and bounded rationality, pioneered by Kahneman, Tversky, and Thaler.
Systematic deviation from rational judgment, such as overconfidence or anchoring, studied extensively in behavioral economics.
Standard neoclassical assumption of consistent, utility-maximizing behavior, challenged by behavioral economics' findings of systematic bias.
Herbert Simon's concept that decision-makers face cognitive and informational limits, leading them to satisfice rather than optimize.
Psychologist who won the economics Nobel for Prospect Theory and cognitive-bias research with Amos Tversky; wrote Thinking, Fast and Slow.
Behavioral economist known for nudge theory, mental accounting, and the endowment effect; won the Nobel Prize in 2017.
Herbert Simon's concept of choosing a good-enough option rather than the optimal one, under bounded rationality.
Daniel Kahneman's 2011 book distinguishing fast, intuitive System 1 thinking from slow, deliberate System 2 reasoning.

sections.name: Financial Economics
sections.blurb: Asset pricing, risk, and the instruments and models—options, bonds, CAPM, Black-Scholes—that govern financial markets.

entries.note: Uncertainty over financial outcomes, measured via variance and volatility and priced through models like CAPM and the Sharpe ratio.
Derivative contracts giving the right, not obligation, to buy or sell an asset; priced using the Black-Scholes model.
Simultaneous buying and selling to exploit price discrepancies risklessly; underlies no-arbitrage asset-pricing models like Black-Scholes.
American economist known for the Fisher equation linking nominal and real interest rates, and the debt-deflation theory of depressions.
Model pricing an asset's expected return via its beta, or sensitivity to non-diversifiable market risk.
Statistical measure of price fluctuation used to gauge risk and as an input to option-pricing models like Black-Scholes.
Formula for pricing European options based on volatility, strike price, and time to expiration, developed by Black, Scholes, and Merton.
Preference for certain outcomes over uncertain ones of equal expected value, formalized via concave utility functions in finance.
Measure of risk-adjusted investment return, dividing excess return over the risk-free rate by volatility; devised by William Sharpe.
Ease of converting an asset to cash without loss of value; liquidity crises can trigger bank runs and crashes.
Situation where depositors withdraw funds en masse fearing insolvency, potentially causing a solvent bank to fail; motivates deposit insurance.
Measure of an asset's sensitivity to overall market risk, used in the Capital Asset Pricing Model to determine expected return.
Full name of the Black-Scholes option-pricing model, extended by Robert Merton, for which Scholes and Merton won the Nobel.
Fixed-income debt securities paying periodic interest, or coupons; prices move inversely to interest rates.
Options-pricing sensitivity measuring how much an option's price changes per unit change in the underlying asset's price.
Debt securities issued by national governments to finance spending, considered relatively low-risk fixed-income instruments.
Loans secured by real estate; mortgage-backed securities and subprime lending were central to the 2008 financial crisis.
Collections of financial assets held by an investor; diversification across a portfolio reduces idiosyncratic risk.
Nobel economist known for research on asset-price volatility, behavioral finance, and the Case-Shiller housing price index.
Owners of a corporation's equity, whose interests corporate-finance theory, including Modigliani-Miller, studies relative to managers and creditors.
Equity shares representing partial ownership of a corporation, priced in markets partly per models like CAPM.

sections.name: Labor, Welfare & Inequality Economics
sections.blurb: Labor markets, human capital, income distribution, and the empirical methods economists use to measure inequality and welfare.

entries.note: Skills, education, and training embodied in workers that raise productivity and wages; theorized by Gary Becker and Jacob Mincer.
Flow of earnings to households from labor, capital, and transfers; its distribution is central to inequality measurement.
Factor of production supplied by workers; its market clears through wages, subject to unions, minimum wages, and search frictions.
Indian welfare economist known for the capability approach, social choice theory, and work on famine and development.
Legal wage floor debated for effects on employment; David Card's natural-experiment studies challenged simple competitive predictions.
Allocation where no one can be made better off without making someone else worse off; core welfare-economics benchmark.
Statistical methods applying regression, instrumental variables, and natural experiments to test economic theories with real-world data.
Synonym for Pareto efficiency: no reallocation can improve one party without harming another; a central welfare criterion.
French economist whose Capital in the Twenty-First Century argues returns on capital outpace growth, driving rising inequality.
Econometric technique comparing changes over time between treatment and control groups to estimate causal effects, as in minimum-wage studies.
Unequal distribution of income across a population, measured by the Gini coefficient and analyzed by Piketty and Kuznets.
Branch studying how resource allocations affect social well-being, using Pareto efficiency and compensation criteria; Sen and Pigou are key figures.
Labor economist awarded the Nobel for natural-experiment studies of minimum wage and immigration, notably with Alan Krueger in New Jersey.
Disparities in income and wealth across individuals or groups, measured via the Gini coefficient and studied by Piketty.
Chicago economist who extended economic analysis to human capital, discrimination, crime, and the family.
Statistical measure of income or wealth inequality ranging from 0, perfect equality, to 1, perfect inequality.
Econometric technique using a variable correlated with treatment but not the outcome directly, to estimate causal effects.
Likely shorthand for Amartya Sen, the Indian welfare economist known for the capability approach and famine studies.
Nobel labor economist known for research on the gender pay gap and women's changing role in the U.S. labor force.
Full name of Gary Becker, Chicago economist who applied economic analysis to human capital, discrimination, and crime.
Anthropometric measure used in economic history to proxy nutrition and living standards, and in labor economics to study wage premiums.
Econometric tool for estimating causal effects when treatment is not randomly assigned, isolating variation uncorrelated with the outcome's error term.
Amount of labor workers offer at each wage, modeled via the tradeoff between income from work and leisure.
Worker organizations that bargain collectively over wages and conditions, studied for effects on wage compression and employment.
Empirical research design exploiting real-world events that mimic random assignment, as in David Card's minimum-wage studies.
State of lacking sufficient income or resources; measured via poverty lines and studied in development and welfare economics.
Statistical technique estimating relationships between variables, foundational to empirical labor and applied microeconomics.

sections.name: International Trade & Development Economics
sections.blurb: Comparative advantage, exchange rates, and the theories explaining trade flows and economic development across nations.

entries.note: British classical economist who formulated comparative advantage, the labor theory of value, and a theory of land rent.
Nobel laureate known for New Trade Theory explaining intra-industry trade via economies of scale, and for economic geography.
Exchange of goods and services across borders, explained by comparative advantage, factor endowments, and increasing-returns trade models.
Ricardian principle that countries gain from specializing in goods with lower opportunity cost, even without absolute advantage.
Difference between a country's exports and imports of goods and services; surpluses and deficits reflect trade competitiveness.
Price of one currency in terms of another, determined by trade flows, interest-rate differentials, and purchasing power parity.
Currency prices that adjust to trade and capital flows; fixed, floating, and managed regimes shape international monetary policy.
Increasing integration of world trade, capital, and labor markets, debated for effects on inequality and national sovereignty.
Development economist known for shock-therapy transition advice to post-Soviet economies and work on ending extreme poverty.
Relative currency values that adjust to trade flows and interest-rate differentials, central to open-economy macroeconomics.
Trade theory explaining that countries export goods intensive in their abundant factors of production, whether land, labor, or capital.
Small loans to poor entrepreneurs lacking collateral, popularized by Muhammad Yunus's Grameen Bank as a development tool.
Sector central to development economics, including farm subsidies, commodity markets, and rural poverty in developing economies.
Development economist known for exit, voice, and loyalty and the hiding hand concept of unplanned project benefits.
Subfield studying how poor countries grow, industrialize, and raise living standards, using tools from microfinance to aid-effectiveness studies.
Transfers of money or resources from richer to poorer nations intended to spur development, debated for effectiveness by Sachs and critics.
Peruvian economist who argued that formalizing property rights for the poor unlocks dead capital and spurs development.
Condition stating a currency devaluation improves the trade balance only if export and import demand elasticities together exceed one.
Purchasing power parity: theory that exchange rates adjust so identical goods cost the same across countries.
Exchange of goods and services across borders or markets, explained by comparative advantage and factor-endowment theories.
Economist who developed input-output analysis of inter-industry production and identified the Leontief paradox in U.S. trade patterns.

appendix.answer: "The Market for 'Lemons': Quality Uncertainty and the Market Mechanism"
"The Market for Lemons: Quality Uncertainty and the Market Mechanism"
The Calculus of Consent: Logical Foundations of Constitutional Democracy
The General Theory of Employment, Interest, and Money
